title: Expressing support for the goals of a "NICU Baby's Bill of Rights".
plain_summary: This bill supports a special set of rights for babies in hospital intensive care units and their families. It wants hospitals to include parents more in making medical decisions about their babies and to better explain treatment choices to families. The bill also calls for better planning when babies are ready to go home from the hospital and more mental health support for families during this stressful time. The goal is to make sure families get the help and information they need when their baby is in intensive care.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title and Background
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for the goals of a NICU Baby's Bill of Rights. A NICU is a neonatal intensive care unit, which is a special hospital area that cares for newborn babies who are very sick or were born too early. The resolution explains that more than three hundred eighty thousand babies are born too early each year in the United States. Many other babies are born on time but have serious medical problems that require NICU care. Black babies are born too early fifty percent more often than white babies. Being born too early is the top cause of death for children under one year old. Babies born too early or with medical problems can develop serious health issues like brain damage, lung problems, and eye problems that may last their whole lives or cause death. When a baby needs NICU care, it is very stressful and traumatic for families. Parents often cannot stay close to their sick babies, which makes bonding harder and can hurt the baby's brain development and the parents' mental health. Skin-to-skin contact between babies and parents helps with breastfeeding, emotional bonding, brain development, and helps parents feel more confident caring for their baby. When NICU families get good support, both babies and families do better. All families deserve care that focuses on their needs and helps their baby develop properly. NICU families should get personalized support, education, and resources they need during their NICU stay and after going home. Many NICU families are not fully told about or asked for their input on their baby's care, including treatment plans, how sick their baby really is, and what the baby should eat. Studies show that human breast milk is best for babies, but many families do not know about options like donated human milk from other mothers. All NICU families have the right to be fully informed and give permission for all parts of their baby's care.

sections.title: House Resolution Actions
sections.text: The House of Representatives takes these actions. First, it supports the goals of a NICU Baby's Bill of Rights that helps NICU families and their babies. Second, it asks doctors, hospitals, and other healthcare workers to use a family-centered approach to NICU care. This means focusing on what babies and families need and letting parents make the right decisions for their situation. It also means encouraging parents to help take care of their babies as much as possible every day. Third, it recognizes that parents are the best people to speak up for their NICU baby. It asks hospitals to create clear rules that make sure parents or main caregivers can be present, involved, and work together with hospital staff. Hospitals should include family support groups when creating these rules from the very beginning. Fourth, it encourages hospitals to use an informed consent model for important care decisions for all NICU babies. This includes treatment plans and what the baby should eat. Families should be fully told about the risks and benefits of all care choices available. This helps parents make informed decisions for their child. Fifth, it emphasizes how important it is to have a complete discharge planning process that includes family participation. The plan should be made specifically for each individual baby and family's needs. Sixth, it stresses how important mental health support is for parents and family caregivers of NICU babies. This includes connecting them to peer support programs where they can talk to other NICU parents, support groups, and counseling. These services can be in person or online and help reduce the effects of mood and anxiety problems that can happen during this difficult time.
